articles.title: About this site
articles.contents:
Owen Graham


Home
About
Data Visualisations
Using text as data
☰


  
    
      
        
        
        
      
      
    
    
      
  Home


  About


  Data Visualisations


  Using text as data

      
  






About this site
This website is a showcase of coding and data
visualisations as a part of my GV300 module at the University of
Essex!




Over the duration of my course I have learnt numerous functions of
the Rstudio environment. I have gained experience in how to create and
analyse data visualisations, looking specifically on how to create and
analyse Linear, Choice and Measurement models and applying them to real
world examples. We learnt theories such as IRT to gain an insight into
how reliability and bias can effect the different models and data
measurements. Another method I have learnt is the text as data approach,
such as the Naive Bayes example, which has given me great insight into
how to handle high-dimensional data in the form of text.

articles.title: Owen Graham GV300 Assignment 4
articles.contents:
Owen Graham


Home
About
Data Visualisations
Using text as data
☰


  
    
      
        
        
        
      
      
    
    
      
  Home


  About


  Data Visualisations


  Using text as data

      
  






Owen Graham GV300 Assignment 4
Crossexamining valid votes in individual
constituencies with turnout and location.




In this work I am showcasing here is derived from the dataset of the
2024 election results, looking specifically at the number of valid votes
in individual constituencies, cross examining it with the local turnout
and constituency location.
First the packages and dataset used must be loaded
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
uk_csv <- "https://electionresults.parliament.uk/general-elections/6/candidacies.csv"
uk_raw <- readr::read_csv(uk_csv, show_col_types = FALSE)
names(uk_raw) <- names(uk_raw) %>% tolower() %>% str_replace_all("\\s+", "_")
Then I cleaned up the data set
uk1 <- uk_raw %>%
  filter(!`election_is_by-election`)

uk2 <- uk1 %>%
  select(
    constituency = constituency_name,
    country      = country_name,
    valid_votes  = candidate_vote_count,
    electorate   = electorate
  )

uk3 <- uk2 %>%
  group_by(country, constituency) %>%
  summarise(
    valid_votes = sum(valid_votes, na.rm = TRUE),
    electorate  = max(electorate, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

uk <- uk3 %>%
  mutate(turnout_registered = 100 * valid_votes / electorate) %>%
  filter(is.finite(turnout_registered), electorate > 0)
This makes it easier to use for the following code chunk and data
visualisation.
The following is a code chunk example:
smallest <- uk %>% 
  arrange(valid_votes) %>% 
  slice_head(n = 5) %>%
  select(constituency, country, valid_votes, turnout_registered)
smallest
## # A tibble: 5 × 4
##   constituency              country  valid_votes turnout_registered
##   <chr>                     <chr>          <dbl>              <dbl>
## 1 Na h-Eileanan an Iar      Scotland       13528               63.4
## 2 Orkney and Shetland       Scotland       20688               60.4
## 3 Manchester Rusholme       England        29033               40.0
## 4 Kingston upon Hull East   England        29816               42.2
## 5 Blaenau Gwent and Rhymney Wales          29922               42.7
largest <- uk %>% 
  arrange(desc(valid_votes)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 5) %>%
  select(constituency, country, valid_votes, turnout_registered)

largest
## # A tibble: 5 × 4
##   constituency         country valid_votes turnout_registered
##   <chr>                <chr>         <dbl>              <dbl>
## 1 Rushcliffe           England       57744               72.9
## 2 Winchester           England       57076               72.9
## 3 North East Hampshire England       55560               72.2
## 4 Horsham              England       55499               70.1
## 5 Stroud               England       55221               70.9
This first chunk of simple code arranged the dataset into ascending
order of valid votes, taking the top 5 constituencies in terms of lowest
number of valid votes. It presents its name, country, number of valid
votes and registered turnout percentage. The second chunk then arranged
the dataset into the inverse, presenting the top 5 constituencies in
terms of highest number of valid votes.
This is important to show as it gives us a better idea of where
greater voter inequality might be taking place. It gives us an idea of
where the smallest constituencies whilst also presenting how low turnout
can get in those areas.
The Following is a Data Visualisation example:
ggplot(uk, aes(x = country, y = valid_votes, fill = country)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Constituency Sizes by Country",
    y = "Valid Votes",
    x = "Country",
    fill = "Country"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() 

This data visualisation shows the distribution of constituency sizes
by country within the UK. I use the ggplot function to create a
visualisation of the previously created uk data set, opting to use a
boxplot. The boxplot is used as it shows the median, interquartile range
as well as outliers best. I then used scale_y_continuous and
labels=scale::comma to format and modify the y-axis to better the data,
with all values coming within a very particular range. I then set the
colour palette to better present the data and labelled each axis and
titled the visualisation.
The resulting visualisation I believe creates a good clear
representation of the data. It shows that Northern Ireland has the most
equal-sized constituencies, that Scotland has two major outliers but
without them it is England that has the far most inequality in terms of
constituency size.
Another way of creating a visualisation for this is through a simple
histogram with an overall mean integrated.
overall_mean <- mean(uk$valid_votes)

ggplot(uk, aes(x = valid_votes)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = overall_mean, 
             color = "red", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ country, ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Constituency Sizes by Country",
    subtitle = "Red line shows overall UK mean",
    x = "Number of Valid Votes",
    y = "Count of Constituencies"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

However, due to the large difference in number of constituencies
between countries, I found this visualisation to be more difficult to
read and the former example to be cleaner, however should you want to
compare the average mean across all countries perhaps this is the better
method.

articles.title: Text as Data Showcase
articles.contents:
Owen Graham


Home
About
Data Visualisations
Using text as data
☰


  
    
      
        
        
        
      
      
    
    
      
  Home


  About


  Data Visualisations


  Using text as data

      
  






Text as Data Showcase
Using Text as Data methods such as naive bayes to
understand the varying length and nature of Presidential election
concession speeches.




The work I am showing here will focus on the Naive Bayes example with
Quanteda, which manages to turn text into a form of data.
First, I must load the required packages for the data process:
library(stringr)
library(car)
library(dplyr)
library(quanteda)
library(readtext)
library(quanteda.textmodels)
Then I load in the data and turn it into a corpus object using
quanteda. This will turn it into a more structured collection of text
documents that can be analysed.
speeches <- readtext("Concession Speeches/*.txt")  
speeches_corpus <- corpus(speeches) 
summary(speeches_corpus)  
## Corpus consisting of 19 documents, showing 19 documents:
## 
##                  Text Types Tokens Sentences
##      Bryan_1896_D.txt    43     51         2
##       Bush_1992_R.txt   308    784        57
##     Carter_1980_D.txt   317    752        37
##    Clinton_2016_D.txt   473   1443        55
##      Dewey_1948_R.txt    52     62         2
##       Dole_1996_R.txt   362   1231        69
##    Dukakis_1988_D.txt   432   1287        59
##       Ford_1976_R.txt   243    518        22
##  Goldwater_1964_R.txt   311    783        29
##       Gore_2000_D.txt   473   1206        57
##     Hoover_1932_R.txt    58     77         4
##   Humphrey_1968_D.txt   289    694        43
##      Kerry_2004_D.txt   578   1867       104
##     McCain_2008_R.txt   490   1483        78
##   McGovern_1972_D.txt   347    811        37
##    Mondale_1984_D.txt   302    818        57
##      Nixon_1960_R.txt   295   1005        48
##     Romney_2012_R.txt   292    814        54
##  Stevenson_1952_D.txt   219    425        24
Tokens meaning the word count, and types meaning the number of
different words used.
A major component of this research comes from the different examples,
in this case concession speeches, that we are observing. So we then take
a look at the different examples present.
nms <-names(speeches_corpus) 
nms
##  [1] "Bryan_1896_D.txt"     "Bush_1992_R.txt"      "Carter_1980_D.txt"   
##  [4] "Clinton_2016_D.txt"   "Dewey_1948_R.txt"     "Dole_1996_R.txt"     
##  [7] "Dukakis_1988_D.txt"   "Ford_1976_R.txt"      "Goldwater_1964_R.txt"
## [10] "Gore_2000_D.txt"      "Hoover_1932_R.txt"    "Humphrey_1968_D.txt" 
## [13] "Kerry_2004_D.txt"     "McCain_2008_R.txt"    "McGovern_1972_D.txt" 
## [16] "Mondale_1984_D.txt"   "Nixon_1960_R.txt"     "Romney_2012_R.txt"   
## [19] "Stevenson_1952_D.txt"
Then, we are able to separate out the data into the categories of
different concession speeches.
speeches_corpus$Dates  <- paste(as.numeric(gsub('[^[:digit:]]','', names(speeches_corpus)))) 
speeches_corpus$Party <- str_sub(nms, start= -5, -5) 
speeches_corpus$PNames <- str_sub(nms, end = -12) 
summary(speeches_corpus)
## Corpus consisting of 19 documents, showing 19 documents:
## 
##                  Text Types Tokens Sentences Dates Party    PNames
##      Bryan_1896_D.txt    43     51         2  1896     D     Bryan
##       Bush_1992_R.txt   308    784        57  1992     R      Bush
##     Carter_1980_D.txt   317    752        37  1980     D    Carter
##    Clinton_2016_D.txt   473   1443        55  2016     D   Clinton
##      Dewey_1948_R.txt    52     62         2  1948     R     Dewey
##       Dole_1996_R.txt   362   1231        69  1996     R      Dole
##    Dukakis_1988_D.txt   432   1287        59  1988     D   Dukakis
##       Ford_1976_R.txt   243    518        22  1976     R      Ford
##  Goldwater_1964_R.txt   311    783        29  1964     R Goldwater
##       Gore_2000_D.txt   473   1206        57  2000     D      Gore
##     Hoover_1932_R.txt    58     77         4  1932     R    Hoover
##   Humphrey_1968_D.txt   289    694        43  1968     D  Humphrey
##      Kerry_2004_D.txt   578   1867       104  2004     D     Kerry
##     McCain_2008_R.txt   490   1483        78  2008     R    McCain
##   McGovern_1972_D.txt   347    811        37  1972     D  McGovern
##    Mondale_1984_D.txt   302    818        57  1984     D   Mondale
##      Nixon_1960_R.txt   295   1005        48  1960     R     Nixon
##     Romney_2012_R.txt   292    814        54  2012     R    Romney
##  Stevenson_1952_D.txt   219    425        24  1952     D Stevenson
The 19 documents given are too small of a dataset so we then use
quanteda to develop the corpus from being document based to sentence
based, keeping the same variables. This will make it easier for us to
spot patterns and form predictions.
speeches_sentences <- corpus_reshape(speeches_corpus, to = "sentences", use_docvars = TRUE) 
summary(speeches_sentences)
## Corpus consisting of 838 documents, showing 100 documents:
## 
##                  Text Types Tokens Sentences Dates Party  PNames
##    Bryan_1896_D.txt.1    33     36         1  1896     D   Bryan
##    Bryan_1896_D.txt.2    14     15         1  1896     D   Bryan
##     Bush_1992_R.txt.1    25     29         1  1992     R    Bush
##     Bush_1992_R.txt.2     3      3         1  1992     R    Bush
##     Bush_1992_R.txt.3     6      7         1  1992     R    Bush
##     Bush_1992_R.txt.4     9     10         1  1992     R    Bush
##     Bush_1992_R.txt.5     3      3         1  1992     R    Bush
##     Bush_1992_R.txt.6     5      5         1  1992     R    Bush
##     Bush_1992_R.txt.7     6      7         1  1992     R    Bush
##     Bush_1992_R.txt.8     3      3         1  1992     R    Bush
##     Bush_1992_R.txt.9     5      5         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.10     5      5         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.11     5      5         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.12     3      3         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.13     7      7         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.14     7      7         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.15     9      9         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.16    15     19         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.17     9     10         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.18    14     14         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.19     7      7         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.20     9      9         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.21    23     25         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.22    15     15         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.23    13     13         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.24    19     27         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.25    12     13         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.26    12     16         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.27    11     11         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.28    30     38         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.29    22     24         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.30    11     11         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.31    16     19         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.32    11     12         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.33    30     35         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.34    11     13         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.35    24     30         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.36    17     17         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.37    13     13         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.38    23     31         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.39    20     23         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.40    16     16         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.41    10     10         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.42    15     15         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.43    14     14         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.44     9      9         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.45    24     26         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.46    15     17         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.47    17     19         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.48    17     19         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.49     5      5         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.50     5      5         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.51     5      5         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.52    16     20         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.53    24     27         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.54     9      9         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.55     6      7         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.56     5      5         1  1992     R    Bush
##    Bush_1992_R.txt.57     3      3         1  1992     R    Bush
##   Carter_1980_D.txt.1    33     39         1  1980     D  Carter
##   Carter_1980_D.txt.2    13     13         1  1980     D  Carter
##   Carter_1980_D.txt.3    29     41         1  1980     D  Carter
##   Carter_1980_D.txt.4    26     29         1  1980     D  Carter
##   Carter_1980_D.txt.5    21     24         1  1980     D  Carter
##   Carter_1980_D.txt.6    14     14         1  1980     D  Carter
##   Carter_1980_D.txt.7     8      8         1  1980     D  Carter
##   Carter_1980_D.txt.8    10     11         1  1980     D  Carter
##   Carter_1980_D.txt.9    15     15         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.10    15     16         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.11    22     25         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.12    18     20         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.13     7      7         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.14    20     21         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.15     9      9         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.16    33     43         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.17    16     16         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.18     8      8         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.19    16     19         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.20     9      9         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.21     6      6         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.22    14     15         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.23    23     24         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.24    14     14         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.25    28     32         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.26    33     36         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.27    26     29         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.28    24     28         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.29    14     21         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.30    31     41         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.31    19     21         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.32    12     13         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.33    23     30         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.34    10     10         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.35    17     21         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.36    17     19         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Carter_1980_D.txt.37     5      5         1  1980     D  Carter
##  Clinton_2016_D.txt.1    41     49         1  2016     D Clinton
##  Clinton_2016_D.txt.2     4      4         1  2016     D Clinton
##  Clinton_2016_D.txt.3     9     12         1  2016     D Clinton
##  Clinton_2016_D.txt.4     3      3         1  2016     D Clinton
Note: this does not mean all these documents only have one sentence
but rather than we have now organised these documents into single
sentences.
This can be useful in analysing speech patterns of those that lost
Presidential elections and looking into their reaction.
I then went on to observe the potential political party imbalance in
this data.
table(speeches_sentences$Party)
## 
##   D   R 
## 475 363
Finding a Democrat party leaning set of data.
This can lead to the classifier over-predicting the Democrat Party
concession speeches.
So we then clean the data, removing punctuation and numbers, adapting
any misspelled words, and excluding dates.
speech_tokens <- tokens(speeches_sentences, remove_punct = TRUE, remove_symbols = TRUE, split_hyphens = TRUE, remove_numbers = TRUE)
speeches_clean <- tokens_wordstem(speech_tokens) 
speeches_clean <- tokens_remove(speeches_clean, pattern = c("applpaus", "applause")) 
speeches_clean <- tokens_select(speeches_clean, c("*st", "*th", "*rd"), selection = "remove") 
speeches_clean <- dfm(speeches_clean)
speeches_clean <- dfm_remove(speeches_clean,
                      stopwords("english"))
speeches_clean <- speeches_clean[, order(featnames(speeches_clean))]
I then use a training, development and testing set. This machine
learning process allows training to fit the mode, develpment to tune the
model for Naive Bayes and the testing to evaluate the final performance
of the dataset. In this example I split it so that 60% is used for the
training set, the next 20% for the development set and the final 20% for
the testing set.
n <- nrow(speeches_clean)
training_end <- round(0.6 * n)
development_end <- round(0.8 * n)
Concession_dfm_training <- speeches_clean[1:training_end, ]
Concession_dfm_development <- speeches_clean[(training_end + 1):development_end, ]
Concession_dfm_testing  <- speeches_clean[(development_end + 1):n, ]
Concession_training_labels <- speeches_sentences$Party[1:training_end]
Concession_development_labels   <- speeches_sentences$Party[(training_end + 1):development_end]
Concession_testing_labels  <- speeches_sentences$Party[(development_end + 1):n]
We can now train the Naive Bayes model using the quanteda data
package.
nb_model <- textmodel_nb(Concession_dfm_training, docvars(Concession_dfm_training, "Party"))
summary(nb_model)
## 
## Call:
## textmodel_nb.dfm(x = Concession_dfm_training, y = docvars(Concession_dfm_training, 
##     "Party"))
## 
## Class Priors:
## (showing first 2 elements)
##   D   R 
## 0.5 0.5 
## 
## Estimated Feature Scores:
##        abil       abl      abov   abraham    abroad   absolut    accept
## D 0.0002519 0.0015113 0.0005038 0.0005038 0.0002519 0.0005038 0.0020151
## R 0.0003561 0.0007123 0.0003561 0.0003561 0.0003561 0.0007123 0.0003561
##      access accomplish    achiev   across     activ    actual        ad
## D 0.0002519  0.0002519 0.0012594 0.003023 0.0002519 0.0005038 0.0002519
## R 0.0003561  0.0003561 0.0007123 0.001425 0.0007123 0.0003561 0.0003561
##         add   address     adequ     adlai administr     admir     admit
## D 0.0005038 0.0010076 0.0002519 0.0002519 0.0007557 0.0002519 0.0005038
## R 0.0007123 0.0003561 0.0003561 0.0003561 0.0017806 0.0003561 0.0003561
##       adult    advanc   adviser    affect    affirm   african    agenda
## D 0.0005038 0.0005038 0.0002519 0.0005038 0.0005038 0.0002519 0.0002519
## R 0.0003561 0.0007123 0.0007123 0.0003561 0.0003561 0.0003561 0.0007123
##         ago    ahead
## D 0.0025189 0.001008
## R 0.0007123 0.001068
The Naive Bayes model is useful as it allows us to analyse words used
in a document effectively. It is able to ignore the ordering of words
and instead focus on which words appear, while allowing for words to be
conditionally independent of eachother.
Here, our result allows for an even assumptive split of democrats and
republicans and how often they may used particular words in their
concession speeches.

articles.title: Owen Graham
articles.contents:
Owen Graham
          
          
          Home
          About
          Data Visualisations
          Using text as data
          ☰
          
          
      
        
          
            Owen Graham
          
          
            
              I am a third year undergraduate student from London
              studying Politics and International Relations (BSc) at the
              University of Essex.
              My primary academic interests include development, both
              on a domestic, even local, as well as international stage,
              and the data analysis behind them. Other interests include
              Corruption and the relationship between Government and the
              media.
              I have achieved First-Class honours in both my first
              and second year at the University of Essex, being placed
              on the Dean’s list in both years and awarded the Young
              Scholars Award.
              Through the course for which I will present some of my
              work from on this site, I have developed excellent
              quantitative research skills, learning extensively how to
              use the RStudio environment. I am also completing the
              Q-Step accreditation alongside my degree in order to
              further my knowledge of data-analysis in the field of
              politics and international relations.
            
            
              I am a third year undergraduate student from London
              studying Politics and International Relations (BSc) at the
              University of Essex.
              My primary academic interests include development, both
              on a domestic, even local, as well as international stage,
              and the data analysis behind them. Other interests include
              Corruption and the relationship between Government and the
              media.
              I have achieved First-Class honours in both my first
              and second year at the University of Essex, being placed
              on the Dean’s list in both years and awarded the Young
              Scholars Award.
              Through the course for which I will present some of my
              work from on this site, I have developed excellent
              quantitative research skills, learning extensively how to
              use the RStudio environment. I am also completing the
              Q-Step accreditation alongside my degree in order to
              further my knowledge of data-analysis in the field of
              politics and international relations.
